key_insight: Residual stress in a thin film can be predicted by treating relaxation as a dislocation pile-up against the film-substrate interface, yielding a stress-gradient model that depends strongly on geometry and the initial stress profile.
engineering_relevance: This provides a mechanics-based way to estimate how thin-film thickness and constraint influence residual stress relaxation, which is useful for reliability and design of constrained coatings and microstructures.
method: Formulate residual stress relaxation using dislocation pile-up theory with screw dislocations at an impenetrable substrate interface.
Link plastic strain to dislocation density to derive a fundamental relation between residual stress and dislocation content.
Solve the resulting singular integro-differential equation numerically by collocation for constant, linear, parabolic, and exponential initial stress profiles.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical solutions for multiple initial stress distributions and shows that the resulting residual stress profiles and dislocation densities vary significantly with film geometry and loading shape.
watch_item: The model is idealized to a thin-film segment and relies on an impenetrable interface assumption, so direct use in real materials may require accounting for interface slip, heterogeneity, and additional defect mechanisms.
paper_title: Residual stress gradient in a thin film within the dislocation pile-up theory
